week_headline: Gut health, brain fog, and diet drinks dominate health headlines as younger adults face rising colorectal cancer rates

claims.verdict: NaturalNews.com is a well-documented source of health misinformation, so treat anything from them with real caution. The actual science on diet soda and brain health is genuinely mixed — some observational studies suggest associations between high artificial sweetener intake and stroke or dementia risk, but no study has established cause and effect. A glass of diet soda now and then is not going to harm your brain, and cherry-picking weak correlations to imply it will is not good science.
claims.verdict_highlight: No proven brain harm from diet soda
sources: Stroke journal (AHA, 2017) – observational link, not causation
Harvard Health Publishing on artificial sweeteners
claims.global_opinion: In the US, the FDA considers approved sweeteners like aspartame safe at normal consumption levels, while the EU's EFSA reached similar conclusions after a 2023 re-evaluation. France has invested in larger cohort studies (NutriNet-Santé) that show soft associations with health outcomes but stop short of causal claims. The WHO issued a 2023 advisory suggesting non-sugar sweeteners may not aid long-term weight management, adding nuance without declaring them dangerous.
claims.brit_take: Britain collectively drank diet fizzy drinks through the 80s and 90s with enormous enthusiasm — if it rewired our brains, we'd probably have noticed by now.
claims.claim: Research Examines Links Between Diet Soda Consumption and Brain Health Outcomes – NaturalNews . com

claims.verdict: It's true that demand for registered nutritionists and dietitians is growing in Ireland and across much of Europe, driven by rising rates of obesity, diabetes, and greater public interest in preventive health. However, 'booming' is doing a lot of heavy lifting here — workforce data from Ireland's HSE and CORU suggest steady growth rather than a dramatic surge, and many nutrition-adjacent job titles are unregulated, meaning anyone can call themselves a nutritionist without formal qualifications. Worth being aware of if you're seeking professional advice.
claims.verdict_highlight: Nutrition jobs growing, but 'booming' overstates it
sources: CORU (Health and Social Care Professionals Council, Ireland)
claims.global_opinion: In the UK, dietitian is a protected title under the Health Professions Order 2001, but 'nutritionist' is not, creating a confusing landscape for consumers. Ireland is working toward similar regulatory clarity through CORU registration. In the US, credentialing varies wildly by state, with some requiring full licensure and others having almost no oversight at all.
claims.brit_take: The UK has the same unregulated 'nutritionist' problem — your gym's Instagram-certified nutrition coach and an NHS dietitian are not the same thing, despite what their bios suggest.
claims.claim: Nutrition Jobs Ireland Are Booming and Most People Have No Idea

claims.verdict: This one is genuinely well supported and worth taking seriously. Multiple major studies, including data from the American Cancer Society and research published in CA: A Cancer Journal for Clinicians, confirm that colorectal cancer incidence in adults under 50 has been rising steadily since the 1990s — even as rates in older adults have fallen. Doctors are not entirely sure why, but diet, sedentary lifestyles, obesity, and changes in gut microbiome health are all under investigation. If you have symptoms like unexplained changes in bowel habits, blood in your stool, or persistent abdominal pain, please don't wait — talk to a doctor regardless of your age.
claims.verdict_highlight: Under-50 colorectal cancer rates are genuinely rising
sources: CA: A Cancer Journal for Clinicians (Siegel et al., 2023)
American Cancer Society – Colorectal Cancer Facts & Figures
Lancet Oncology – early-onset colorectal cancer trends
claims.global_opinion: The US has responded by lowering its recommended screening age from 50 to 45, a move endorsed by the American Cancer Society and the US Preventive Services Task Force. The UK's NHS bowel cancer screening programme currently starts at 50, though calls to lower it are growing louder in clinical circles. Australia also screens from 50 via its National Bowel Cancer Screening Program, but gastroenterologists there are equally vocal about the younger trend.
claims.brit_take: The NHS bowel screening kit that lands on your doormat at 50 is genuinely one of the best things they send you — but the evidence is building that we may need to have that conversation a bit earlier.
claims.claim: Local gastroenterologist warns colorectal cancer is no longer just a disease of older adults
